text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon preparing to resect the distal tail of the pancreas and the spleen en bloc, targeting the tumor and addressing multiple spleen infarctions.

The surgeon begins the operation by cutting the left colic flexure, followed by incising the greater omentum to access the omental bursa. The liver and pancreas are visible, with varicose short gastric arteries originating from the splenic artery in the middle of the frame. The pancreatic serosa is opened.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery positioned above the pancreas and proceeds to dissect beneath the pancreas, revealing the splenic vein.

An ultrasound probe is used to examine the pancreas for tumor presence at the planned resection site. The surgeon cuts and ligates the splenic artery using an endogia stapler, which places two triple rows of staples and cuts the tissue between them. The splenic vein is similarly cut and ligated.

The surgeon uses a larger version of the endo-GIA stapler to divide the pancreas in tumor-free tissue. The distal end of the pancreas, containing the tumor, is visible on the right side of the frame. Varicose arteries are cut using the endo-GIA stapler.

The stomach is visible to the left of the frame. The surgeon dissects the spleen's attachment to the diaphragm, encountering inflammatory adhesions and a poorly defined boundary between the stomach and spleen. After a challenging dissection, the stomach is separated from the spleen and transected using an endo-GIA stapler.
The surgeon cuts the specimen loose and places it into an endo-catch bag. The bag is then extracted through an extended umbilical incision.

The staple sutures are visible on the remaining parts of the pancreas and stomach, with the liver positioned at the top of the frame. A TACO cell surgical patch is applied over the remaining pancreas.
The surgeon places a surgical drain in the abdominal cavity before proceeding to close the incision sites.
The surgeon cuts open the pancreas, revealing a solid yellowish tumor within the tissue.
